section.title: Paul before Agrippa
section.sectionRationale: This is the fullest and most personal of Paul's defenses, delivered before a Jewish king who knows the Scriptures. Paul retells his conversion and commission on the Damascus road, presses the risen Christ upon Agrippa from Moses and the Prophets, and turns the dock into a pulpit. It is the climactic testimony of Acts, the gospel proclaimed to the highest rank Paul has yet faced.
section.thesis: Standing in chains before a king, Paul tells how the risen Jesus met him, turned a persecutor into a witness, and sent him to open blind eyes among Jew and Gentile; and he begs even his judges to believe the prophets and become what he is, minus only the chains.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Paul's respectful opening and his hope in God's promise; his account of persecuting the church and meeting the risen Christ on the Damascus road; his faithful obedience to the heavenly vision, preaching light to Jew and Gentile; and the exchange with Festus and Agrippa that ends in a verdict of innocence.

units.label: Paul's opening and his hope
units.note: on trial for the promise made to the fathers

verses.text: Agrippa said to Paul, “You have permission to speak for yourself.” Then Paul stretched out his hand and began his defense:
“King Agrippa, I consider myself fortunate to stand before you today to defend myself against all the accusations of the Jews,
especially since you are acquainted with all the Jewish customs and controversies. I beg you, therefore, to listen to me patiently.
Surely all the Jews know how I have lived from my earliest childhood among my own people, and also in Jerusalem.
They have known me for a long time and can testify, if they are willing, that I lived as a Pharisee, adhering to the strictest sect of our religion.
And now I stand on trial because of my hope in the promise that God made to our fathers,
the promise our twelve tribes are hoping to see fulfilled as they earnestly serve God day and night. It is because of this hope, O king, that I am accused by the Jews.
Why would any of you consider it incredible that God raises the dead?

units.label: The persecutor meets the risen Christ
units.note: Saul, why do you persecute Me

verses.text: So then, I too was convinced that I ought to do all I could to oppose the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
And that is what I did in Jerusalem. With authority from the chief priests I put many of the saints in prison, and when they were condemned to death, I cast my vote against them.
I frequently had them punished in the synagogues, and I tried to make them blaspheme. In my raging fury against them, I even went to foreign cities to persecute them.
In this pursuit I was on my way to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests.
About noon, O king, as I was on the road, I saw a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me and my companions.
We all fell to the ground, and I heard a voice say to me in Hebrew, ‘Saul, Saul, why do you persecute Me? It is hard for you to kick against the {{kentron|goads}}.’
‘Who are You, Lord?’ I asked. ‘I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting,’ the Lord replied.
‘But get up and stand on your feet. For I have appeared to you to appoint you as a servant and as a {{martys|witness}} of what you have seen from Me and what I will show you.
I will rescue you from your own people and from the Gentiles. I am sending you to them

to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those sanctified by faith in Me.’

units.label: Not disobedient to the vision
units.note: light to our people and to the Gentiles

verses.text: So then, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision.

First to those in Damascus and Jerusalem, then to everyone in the region of Judea, and then to the Gentiles, I declared that they should {{metanoeo|repent}} and turn to God, performing deeds worthy of their repentance.
For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple courts and tried to kill me.
But I have had God’s help to this day, and I stand here to testify to small and great alike. I am saying nothing beyond what the prophets and Moses said would happen:
that the Christ would suffer, and as the first to rise from the dead, would proclaim light to our people and to the Gentiles.”

units.label: Almost persuaded
units.note: would to God you were as I am, except these chains

verses.text: At this stage of Paul’s defense, Festus exclaimed in a loud voice, “You are insane, Paul! Your great learning is driving you to madness!”
But Paul answered, “I am not insane, most excellent Festus; I am speaking words of truth and sobriety.
For the king knows about these matters, and I can speak freely to him. I am confident that none of this has escaped his notice, because it was not done in a corner.
King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you do.”
Then Agrippa said to Paul, “Can you persuade me in such a short time to become a {{christianos|Christian}}?”
“Short time or long,” Paul replied, “I wish to God that not only you but all who hear me this day may become what I am, except for these chains.”
Then the king and the governor rose, along with Bernice and those seated with them.
On their way out, they said to one another, “This man has done nothing worthy of death or imprisonment.”
And Agrippa said to Festus, “This man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar.”

kentron.gloss: A goad, the sharp point used to drive oxen; also a sting. To kick against the goads was a proverb for fighting a stronger power to one's own hurt. The risen Christ tells Saul that his fierce campaign has really been a futile struggle against God, wounding only himself.

martys.gloss: A witness, one who testifies to what he has seen. Christ appoints Paul a martys of the risen Lord, the very calling given to the apostles in Acts 1:8. From this word comes martyr, for faithful witness would cost many their lives; Paul's whole ministry is summed up as bearing witness to Jesus.

metanoeo.gloss: To repent, literally to change one's mind, a turning of the whole person from sin to God. Paul's message everywhere is that people should repent and turn to God and show it by their deeds. Repentance is not mere regret but a reorientation of life that follows the opening of blind eyes.

christianos.gloss: A Christian, one belonging to Christ; a name first given at Antioch, Acts 11:26. Agrippa uses it half in irony, asking whether Paul thinks he can make him a Christian so quickly. Paul takes the word in earnest and wishes all his hearers were as he is, except for his chains.

basic.context: Festus has arranged for Paul to speak before King Agrippa, an expert in Jewish matters, so he can frame the charges for Caesar. Given the floor, Paul delivers the longest and most personal of his defenses. He describes his zealous past as a persecutor, tells how the risen Jesus stopped him on the Damascus road and commissioned him, and then appeals directly to Agrippa to believe the Scriptures and the risen Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as testimony that becomes evangelism. Paul moves from his life story to the Damascus-road encounter to a direct gospel appeal. Watch how the defense quietly puts the king on trial: by the end it is Agrippa, not Paul, who must decide what to do with the risen Jesus and the prophets he claims to believe.
basic.originalLanguages: Christ tells Saul it is hard to kick against the kentron, the goads, resisting God only wounds himself. He appoints Paul a martys, a witness, of the risen Lord. Paul's message is that all should metanoeo, repent, and turn to God. And Agrippa asks whether Paul would so quickly make him a Christianos, a Christian.
basic.typology: The suffering and rising Christ Paul preaches is the fulfillment of Moses and the Prophets: the Messiah must suffer, be the first to rise, and bring light to the nations. Paul's commission to open blind eyes and turn people from darkness to light echoes the servant of Isaiah, a light for the Gentiles.
basic.christ: The risen Jesus is the whole content of Paul's defense. He appears in blazing light, names Himself, and sends Paul to the nations. The gospel Paul preaches is exactly this: the Christ would suffer, rise first from the dead, and proclaim light to Jew and Gentile. The persecuted one is the reigning Lord.
basic.god: God had promised light for the nations through Israel, and here that promise is being fulfilled. The same God who made the promise to the fathers now sends Paul to open Gentile eyes. God's help sustains His witness to this day, and God's purpose cannot be stopped by prison or a king's hesitation.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 9 and 22 give the other two accounts of the Damascus road. Isaiah 42 speaks of the servant as a light to the Gentiles. Acts 1:8 sets out the call to be witnesses. Luke 24:46 to 47 states the pattern: the Christ must suffer, rise, and repentance be preached to all nations.
basic.oneStory: The commission Paul received is the mission of the whole book: from Jerusalem the risen Christ sends His witnesses to open the eyes of the nations. Standing before a king, Paul embodies that mission, preaching light to Jew and Gentile and pleading for all to turn and believe.

context.literary: This is the last and grandest of Paul's five defense speeches in Acts and the third telling of his conversion, given now in the fullest form. Luke places it before royalty and dignitaries in a great audience hall, the high point of Paul's testimony before the powerful. The speech turns from autobiography to direct appeal, so that the defendant becomes the evangelist and the judges become the judged.
context.historical: Herod Agrippa the second was the great-grandson of Herod the Great, ruler of a northern kingdom and custodian of the Jerusalem temple, with authority to appoint the high priest. He knew Jewish law and expectation intimately, which is why Festus sought his help. Bernice was his sister. The setting is the audience hall in Caesarea, filled with military commanders and leading citizens.
context.canonical: The speech gathers the threads of Acts: the risen Christ, the commission to the Gentiles, and the message drawn from Moses and the Prophets that the Messiah must suffer and rise. It fulfills the promise that Paul would bear Christ's name before kings, Acts 9:15, and echoes the servant songs of Isaiah about light for the nations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present the fullest account of Paul's conversion and commission, to show the gospel proclaimed boldly before the highest authority, and to demonstrate again that Paul, and the message he carries, is innocent of any crime.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Paul's unique apostolic call and a particular royal hearing. It does not prescribe an identical experience for every believer. The abiding truths are the risen Christ's power to save even His enemies, the call to bear witness, and the summons to all to repent and turn to God.

debates.question: Was the Damascus road primarily Paul's conversion or his prophetic commissioning?

views.label: Chiefly a conversion
views.case: The account describes a real turning: the arch-persecutor is confronted by the living Christ, learns he has been fighting God, and is changed from enemy to servant; the language of turning from darkness to light and receiving forgiveness of sins, verse 18, describes salvation, and Paul elsewhere counts himself the chief of sinners saved by grace, 1 Timothy 1:13 to 16.

views.label: Chiefly a commissioning
views.case: The emphasis in this telling falls on being appointed a servant and witness and sent to the Gentiles, verses 16 to 18, echoing the call of the prophets like Isaiah and Jeremiah; on this reading the encounter is above all Paul's ordination to apostolic mission rather than a template of personal conversion.

debates.bearing: The two are not opposed: the same encounter both saved Paul and sent him. Holding them together guards against reducing the event either to a private conversion story or to a mere ministry appointment; the risen Christ who saves also commissions, and the grace that turns an enemy also enlists him for the mission to the nations.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary carries the drama of grace. The risen Lord tells Saul it is hard to kick against the kentron, the goads, his violent resistance only injures himself and cannot defeat God. Christ appoints him a martys, a witness, the same office given the apostles, and the root from which the word martyr would grow. Paul's whole message is a call to metanoeo, to repent, to change the mind and turn the life to God. And when Agrippa speaks of becoming a Christianos, a Christian, Paul seizes the word in earnest longing for the king's salvation.

typology.type: The servant who is a light to the Gentiles
typology.fulfillment: Paul's commission to open blind eyes and turn the nations from darkness to light fulfills the servant of Isaiah 42:6 to 7, ultimately realized in Christ and extended through His witnesses.

typology.type: The suffering and rising Messiah of Moses and the Prophets
typology.fulfillment: Paul says he preaches nothing beyond what the Scriptures foretold: the Christ would suffer and be the first to rise, fulfilled in Jesus, Luke 24:46.
Paul's call, get up and stand on your feet, for I am sending you, echoes the commissioning of prophets like Ezekiel and Jeremiah, now fulfilled in the apostolic mission to the nations.

study.christ: Jesus is the beginning, center, and goal of the speech. He is the risen Lord who blazes from heaven, names Himself to His persecutor, and sends him to the nations. He is the Christ of whom Moses and the Prophets spoke, who must suffer, rise first from the dead, and proclaim light to Jew and Gentile. To turn from darkness to light and receive forgiveness of sins is to come to Him. Paul's plea to Agrippa is finally a plea to receive this Jesus.
study.god: God stands behind the whole story: the promise made to the fathers, the sending of Paul, the help that has sustained him to this day. The God of Israel is fulfilling His ancient word by bringing light to the Gentiles through His risen Son. His saving purpose reaches even a raging persecutor and presses now upon a king, for God desires that all should repent and turn to Him and live.

crossReferences.note: The first account of the Damascus-road encounter, the conversion and commissioning of Saul.
The servant as a light for the Gentiles, to open blind eyes, echoed in Paul's commission.
The Christ must suffer, rise, and repentance for forgiveness be preached to all nations, the pattern Paul proclaims.
Paul the former persecutor saved as the foremost of sinners, a display of Christ's patience.

study.oneStory: The ascended Christ commissions witnesses to carry His light from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, and here that commission is on full display. A once-violent enemy, seized by grace, stands before a king and preaches the suffering and risen Messiah, the light for Jew and Gentile alike. The gospel presses upward to the highest thrones, pleading with all who hear to turn and be saved.

quiz.q: What did the risen Jesus commission Paul to do?

options.text: To be a witness who would open blind eyes and turn Jew and Gentile from darkness to light.
options.note: Christ appoints Paul a servant and witness, sending him to open eyes and turn people to God, verses 16 to 18.

options.text: To rescue him from his enemies and keep him safe.
options.note: Christ does promise to rescue him, verse 17, but that is in service of the mission, not the commission itself.

options.text: To rebuild the temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: Nothing of the kind is said; Paul is sent to the nations with the gospel, not to a building project.

options.text: To keep his new faith private and cause no trouble.
options.note: The opposite; he is publicly sent to Jew and Gentile as a witness to the risen Christ.

quiz.q: What does Christ mean by telling Saul it is hard to kick against the goads?

options.text: His fierce fight against Jesus is a futile struggle against God that only wounds himself.
options.note: The proverb pictures an ox harming itself by kicking the goad; Saul cannot defeat God by persecuting the church.

options.text: Saul has been feeling guilty and resisting his conscience.
options.note: There may be an inner struggle in view, but the saying is chiefly about the futility of fighting the living God.

options.text: Saul should stop traveling to distant cities.
options.note: It is not about his travels but about the hopelessness of opposing Christ.

options.text: Saul is being praised for his zeal.
options.note: It is a rebuke, not praise; his zeal has been aimed against God Himself.

quiz.q: What is the heart of the message Paul says he preaches from Moses and the Prophets?

options.text: That the Christ would suffer, rise first from the dead, and bring light to Jew and Gentile.
options.note: Paul states this as the sum of his message, verses 22 to 23, drawn straight from the Scriptures.

options.text: That people should repent and turn to God and do deeds worthy of repentance.
options.note: True and part of his preaching, verse 20, but the core content is the suffering and risen Christ for all peoples.

options.text: That Israel alone would be saved.
options.note: Paul insists the light is for the Gentiles too, not for Israel only.

options.text: That the Messiah would come only to reign, never to suffer.
options.note: Paul says the opposite: the Christ would suffer and be the first to rise.

quiz.q: How does Paul respond to Agrippa's question about becoming a Christian?

options.text: He earnestly wishes that Agrippa and all his hearers might become as he is, except for the chains.
options.note: Paul turns the king's half-ironic question into a heartfelt evangelistic longing, verse 29.

options.text: He appeals to Agrippa's belief in the prophets to persuade him.
options.note: He does appeal to the prophets, verse 27, but his direct answer is the longing that all would be saved.

options.text: He grows angry and rebukes the king for mocking him.
options.note: Paul is gracious, not angry; he responds with warmth and hope, not rebuke.

options.text: He agrees that conversion is impossible in so short a time.
options.note: He says the opposite, longing that short time or long, all might come to Christ.

quiz.q: What verdict did Agrippa and Festus reach about Paul?

options.text: That he had done nothing worthy of death or imprisonment.
options.note: As they left they agreed Paul was innocent, verses 31 to 32, one more declaration of the gospel's innocence.

options.text: That he could have been freed had he not appealed to Caesar.
options.note: Agrippa says this, verse 32, but it flows from the deeper verdict that Paul was innocent.

options.text: That he was guilty but deserved leniency.
options.note: They did not find him guilty at all; they judged him innocent of any crime.

options.text: That he was a dangerous revolutionary.
options.note: They concluded the opposite, that he had done nothing deserving punishment.

questions: The risen Christ met Paul while he was still an enemy and made him a witness. How does it encourage you that Jesus saves and calls people who once opposed Him?
Paul turned his defense into an appeal for others to believe. When you are asked about your faith, do you see it as a chance to bear witness rather than merely to protect yourself?
Christ sent Paul to open blind eyes and turn people from darkness to light. Whose eyes are you longing to see opened, and how might God use you in that?
Agrippa came close, yet stopped short of faith. Where are you tempted to remain almost persuaded rather than fully surrendered to Christ?
Paul could say he had not been disobedient to the heavenly vision. What has God clearly called you to that requires fresh obedience today?

translationNotes.note: The voice spoke in Hebrew, which a footnote notes may mean Aramaic, the everyday language of Judea. The proverb about kicking against the goads, the kentron, was widely known in the ancient world and pictures the futility of resisting a stronger power.

To open their eyes and turn them from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God draws on the language of Isaiah's servant, a light for the Gentiles; the commission describes the effect of the gospel: sight, deliverance, forgiveness, and inheritance.

Agrippa's question is often rendered almost you persuade me to become a Christian, but the Greek more likely carries a note of irony, in such a short time. Either way Paul answers the words in deep earnest, longing for the king's salvation.

Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
